ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00KY (Cakes) is the closest defensible category-level match; no specific chocolate red velvet cake code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical slice of chocolate red velvet cake weighs approximately 80 g, representing a standard single-serve dessert portion as commonly offered in Singapore cafes and bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate Red Velvet Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Chocolate Red Velvet Cake (cr_id G00010544). All per-100g values are as reported. The HPB entry reflects the cake as served without ice cream. Protein is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB; this is noted and justified below.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chocolate Red Velvet Cake in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous food: 'Cake, chocolate, made from packet mix with chocolate frosting' (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The high-sugar, low-fibre profile of this cake (sugar 9.94 g/100g, fibre 0 g/100g) is consistent with a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 72 × 10.36 × 0.80 / 100 = 5.97, rounded to 5.9 for an 80 g slice.

hypertension.key_factor: High free sugar content (9.94 g/100g) with negligible potassium (5 mg/100g) and zero dietary fibre; AHA 2018 evidence links excess free sugar intake to elevated blood pressure independent of sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit consumption of this cake due to its high free sugar load and absence of fibre or potassium; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (≤40 g) and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods at the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar constitutes 96% of total carbohydrate (9.94 g of 10.36 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre, producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; estimated GI of 72 places this in the high-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This cake is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its near-pure sugar carbohydrate profile, high GI (~72), and absence of fibre; if consumed on an exceptional basis, limit to one very small slice (≤40 g) and pair with a protein source to blunt glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cream cheese frosting and butter contribute saturated fat not fully captured in the HPB per-100g data; high free sugar intake is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this cake as an occasional indulgence only, given its high free sugar content and likely saturated fat contribution from cream cheese frosting and butter; portion control to ≤40 g per occasion is advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free sugar (9.94 g/100g) and saturated fat from butter and cream cheese frosting promote pro-inflammatory mediators; artificial red food colouring may trigger sensitivity reactions in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this cake due to its high sugar and saturated fat content, both of which are associated with increased inflammatory burden; artificial food colouring may additionally provoke sensitivity reactions in some patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate Red Velvet Cake is a baked Western-origin layer cake that combines the characteristic deep red or burgundy colour of classic red velvet cake with the addition of cocoa powder for a mild chocolate flavour. The cake layers are made from flour, sugar, cocoa, buttermilk, butter, eggs, red food colouring, baking soda, and vinegar; the acid-base reaction between buttermilk and baking soda produces a tender, moist crumb. The cake is typically finished with a generous layer of cream cheese frosting, which contributes a tangy, rich sweetness that contrasts with the subtle cocoa notes of the sponge. As sold in Singapore cafes and bakeries, it is served as a slice without ice cream. Nutritionally, the HPB Foodo database reports 42 kcal/100g with 10.36 g carbohydrate (of which 9.94 g is sugar) and only 0.25 g fat per 100g; however, these values likely reflect the cake sponge component and may underrepresent the full caloric and fat contribution of the cream cheese frosting layer. Protein is reported as 0 g/100g by HPB, which is atypically low for a cake containing eggs and dairy; this may reflect a specific formulation or a data limitation. Dietary fibre is zero and potassium is negligible (5 mg/100g), making this a nutritionally poor choice for individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 72 (high), based on analogous chocolate cake with frosting data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Common serving context in Singapore includes afternoon tea, birthday celebrations, and cafe dessert menus.

confidence_reason: The HPB Foodo database is an approved primary source for Singapore food records and provides an exact match for this dish (cr_id G00010544). However, confidence is set to medium rather than high because the reported protein value of 0.0 g/100g is atypically low for a cake containing eggs and dairy, suggesting the HPB entry may reflect a specific commercial formulation or have a data limitation. Saturated fat and detailed fatty acid breakdown are not reported by HPB for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement.
notes: 1. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by the HPB Foodo database (confirmed zero per HPB source). This is atypically low for a cake containing eggs and dairy; it may reflect a specific commercial formulation measured by HPB or a data gap. Fat_g of 0.25 g/100g is as reported by HPB and is also unusually low for a frosted cake, likely reflecting the sponge component only. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB — this food contains no meaningful fibre. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB. magnesium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB (0.0 mg/100g). 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1 because these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item. No secondary approved source (USDA FoodData Central) contains an exact match for this specific Singapore commercial chocolate red velvet cake formulation. 3. GI NOTE: GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on 'Cake, chocolate, made from packet mix with chocolate frosting' from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). No direct GI measurement for chocolate red velvet cake was found in any approved GI source. 4. ENERGY NOTE: The reported energy of 42 kcal/100g is very low for a frosted cake and may reflect the cake sponge only or a specific low-calorie commercial formulation. This value is preserved as reported from HPB without modification. 5. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: This is a Western-origin dish sold in Singapore; it does not have established Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi names. Local-language name fields are set to null accordingly. No G6 multilingual obligation applies as this is not classified as a Singaporean dish by cuisine origin.
